description: The provided image is a generic CrossMark 'Check for updates' icon showing a red bookmark badge encircled by a yellow circular arrow on a gray background, rather than an experimental figure from the manuscript. It contains no scientific sub-panels, data curves, spectral peaks, microscopic regions, scale bars, or material labels relevant to the Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steel study. Consequently, no alloy designation, thermomechanical processing condition—such as 1200°C homogenization, hot rolling, or ultrafast cooling at ~40°C/s—phase constituent, or mechanical property metric can be visually extracted or verified. The accompanying text extraction summary reports that the paper investigates Ni and Cr synergistic alloying effects on precipitation control of nanoscale κ-carbides and LRO domains, deformation mechanisms including HDDA and DSBR, and strength–ductility synergy, with supporting data presented in Figs. 4–5 and 10–11. Because the image itself lacks any extractable materials science content, this description relies entirely on the supplied textual context.

Figure 1 presents multi-scale microstructures of four Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C low-density steels: 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr. EBSD IPF maps (a1–d1) and corresponding phase maps (a2–d2) reveal fully recrystallized equiaxed FCC γ-austenite with extensive annealing twins. TEM bright-field images (e–h) confirm the single-phase austenitic matrix. Dark-field images (i–l) captured from {110} reflections show nanoscale ordered features; the caption and associated text report that high-Cr 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels contain homogeneously distributed LRO domains (~1.75 nm and ~2.14 nm), while high-Ni 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steels contain cuboidal κ-carbides (~10.5 nm and ~5.4 nm). Selected-area diffraction patterns along the [011]γ axis (m–p) display superlattice spots indexed to LRO or κ-phase, corroborating these structures. The figure demonstrates that Cr suppresses κ-carbide formation in favor of finer LRO domains, whereas Ni promotes κ-carbide growth, illustrating the synergistic control of ordered nanostructures.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of four Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steels with different alloying additions: 3Cr (black), 2Ni (red), 2Ni3Cr (blue), and 2Ni5Cr (green), all subjected to identical thermomechanical processing and ultrafast cooling. The horizontal axis shows the diffraction angle 2θ from 30° to 100°, and the vertical axis shows intensity in arbitrary units. All four curves display identical sets of diffraction peaks indexed as the face-centered cubic (FCC) γ-austenite phase, specifically the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) reflections, with the γ(111) peak exhibiting the highest intensity. No additional peaks corresponding to body-centered cubic (BCC) ordered B2 phase or other secondary phases are detected in any of the alloys. The citation text confirms that the XRD analysis reveals a single equiaxed FCC γ phase in all samples. This figure supports the core conclusion that varying Ni and Cr contents within this compositional range does not alter the single-phase austenitic structure, establishing the baseline microstructural equivalence for subsequent mechanical property comparisons.

Figure 3 presents high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging of LRO domains and κ-carbides in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C-xNi-yCr austenitic low-density steels. Panels (a), (c), (e), and (g) show atomic-resolution HRTEM micrographs along the [011]γ zone axis for 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr steels, respectively, with insets displaying corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns indexed with γ-matrix and superlattice reflections. Panels (b), (d), (f), and (h) are inverse FFT (IFFT) images obtained using {011} and {100} superlattice reflections to visualize the nanoscale precipitates, where yellow circles highlight ellipsoidal LRO domains or cuboidal κ-carbides. Panel (i) plots the average size measured from over 200 domains, revealing a decreasing trend from ~10.7 nm in 2Ni steel to ~5.5 nm in 2Ni3Cr, ~2.1 nm in 2Ni5Cr, and ~1.75 nm in 3Cr steel. These results demonstrate that Ni addition promotes κ-carbide formation whereas Cr suppresses precipitate growth and favors finer LRO domains, illustrating the critical role of Ni-Cr synergy in tailoring nanoscale precipitation for strength-ductility optimization.

Figure 4 presents the tensile mechanical properties of Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels alloyed with Ni and/or Cr, specifically 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr. Panel (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves, where 2Ni displays the highest yield strength and 3Cr the lowest, while 3Cr achieves the greatest elongation. Panel (b) gives the corresponding true stress-strain curves. Panel (c) plots strain hardening rate versus true strain, with hollow dots marking three distinct stages (I, II, III); the 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr curves (black and green) maintain higher hardening rates in Stages II and III than 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr (red and blue), and increasing Cr content prolongs Stage II while raising Stage III hardening. Panel (d) compares yield strength against the TS×TEL product, positioning the present steels (red stars) above conventional Fe-Al, Fe-Mn, Fe-Mn-C, Fe-Mn-Al-C and stainless steels. The caption and text report that the present alloys achieve superior strength-ductility combinations, exemplified by 3Cr reaching approximately 62.07 GPa·%, and that synergistic Ni and Cr additions enhance yield strength while preserving large total elongation through tuned strain hardening and precipitation behavior.

Figure 5 presents SEM fracture surface micrographs of the fibre zone for four ultrafast-cooled (UFCed) Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steels with different Ni and Cr additions: (a) 3Cr, (b) 2Ni, (c) 2Ni3Cr, and (d) 2Ni5Cr. Each subfigure includes an inset showing the macroscopic fracture surface morphology. All four alloys were processed by homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, hot rolling from 1040–1060°C to 950–970°C with 95% thickness reduction to 4 mm sheets, and online ultrafast cooling at approximately 40°C/s. At a scale bar of 200 μm, the micrographs reveal a dimpled rupture surface across all compositions. The caption and citation state that numerous dimples are observed in the fibre zone of each steel, indicating a ductile fracture mode. No pronounced transition to brittle cleavage or intergranular failure is visually evident among the compared compositions. This figure supports the core conclusion that, despite variations in Ni and Cr content and corresponding differences in tensile properties, all investigated steels maintain ductile fracture characteristics.

This figure presents a comprehensive set of bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns documenting the deformation substructure evolution in Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C-3Cr austenitic low-density steel under progressive tensile strain. Subfigures (a)–(c) at 3% engineering strain reveal strong planar dislocation slip characterized by dislocation multipoles localized within {111} slip bands, dislocations constrained to planar (1-11) slip bands, and interactions between (1-11) and (11-1) slip bands from different slip systems. Subfigures (d)–(f) at 10% strain show increasingly dense and narrower slip bands with elevated dislocation density, including high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA) intersected by multiple planar slip systems. Subfigures (g)–(l) near final failure (LD, ~54% strain) display high-density dislocations, very fine slip bands, nanometre-scale deformation twins (i), and stacking faults (k–l). All images were acquired along the [011]γ zone axis of the FCC matrix, with SAED insets confirming the matrix orientation and defect crystallography. The figure supports the core conclusion that 3Cr steel deformation is dominated by HDDA and dislocation slip, where strain hardening arises primarily from HDDA densification and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR), while deformation twins and stacking faults emerge only at very high strains near fracture with limited contribution to overall ductility.

TEM micrographs showing the deformation-induced microstructural evolution in Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni austenitic low-density steel (2Ni steel) at engineering strains of 5% (a–b), 10% (c–g), and near final failure labeled LD (h–l). At 5% strain, planar slip bands aligned along (111) traces are visible with uniform distribution and SAED insets indexed along the [011] zone axis confirm the FCC austenite matrix. At 10% strain, the slip bands multiply and the inter-band spacing decreases (c–f), with both bright-field and dark-field images revealing (111) trace lines. Near final failure (h–l), the microstructure exhibits a high density of dislocations (h), refined very fine slip bands along (111) traces (i), and stacking faults (j) as indicated by white arrows; dark-field imaging (k) and high-resolution TEM (l, 10 nm scale bar) further characterize the nanoscale defect structure. The figure supports that 2Ni steel deforms predominantly by planar slip and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) accompanied by dynamic recovery, without deformation twins, distinguishing it from the HDDA and twinning-mediated mechanisms reported for 3Cr steel.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of nanoscale κ-carbides in the 2Ni steel (Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni) sampled close to final failure during tensile deformation. Panel (a) is a dark-field TEM image (scale bar 30 nm) showing the distribution and morphology of intragranular κ-carbide precipitates as bright nano-scale cuboidal particles within the γ-austenite matrix. Panel (b) is a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (scale bar 10 nm) revealing the atomic lattice fringes of the κ-carbides, confirming their crystalline ordered structure at near-atomic resolution. These images visually support the caption's identification of κ-carbides in the 2Ni alloy and complement the broader finding that Ni addition promotes κ-carbide formation (average size ~10.5 nm), which are sheared by moving dislocations and contribute to precipitation strengthening via the shearing mechanism. The absence of deformation twins in this steel near failure, as reported in the text, indicates that the strength-ductility balance is governed by dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) and dislocation–precipitate interactions rather than twinning-induced plasticity.

Figure 9 presents first-principles crystal structure models for phases in Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic lightweight steels, calculated via DFT (GGA-PBE). Panel (a) shows the disordered γ-austenite face-centered cubic lattice where Fe/Mn (yellow) and Al (blue) atoms randomly occupy vertex and face-center sites. Panel (b) illustrates the ordered γ-austenite structure with Al exclusively at vertices and Fe/Mn at face centers. Panel (c) depicts the perovskite-type κ-carbide (E2_1 structure) with ideal stoichiometry (Fe,Mn)3AlC, where C (gray) occupies the body-centered octahedral interstitial (1/2,1/2,1/2). Panel (d) displays the Ni-rich κ-carbide variant AlNi3C, with Ni (white) substituting into the structure. The legend identifies yellow as Fe/Mn, white as Ni, blue as Al, and gray as C. These atomic configurations support the thermodynamic analysis that Ni lowers the nucleation energy barrier by stabilizing AlNi3C, promoting κ-carbide formation, whereas Cr raises the barrier, suppressing κ-carbide growth and favoring long-range ordered domains.

Figure 10 presents four histograms showing the precipitation hardening contributions as a function of precipitate radius for (a) 3Cr, (b) 2Ni, (c) 2Ni3Cr, and (d) 2Ni5Cr steels in the Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steel system. The y-axis displays precipitation hardening in MPa and the x-axis shows precipitate radius from 0.5 to 5.0 nm. Green bars denote ordering strengthening (σ_order) and orange bars represent combined coherency and modulus mismatch strengthening (σ_coh+σ_mod). A red dashed line in (a) and (d) indicates the transition radius at which the dominant mechanism shifts from σ_order to σ_coh+σ_mod. The caption states that σ_coh+σ_mod is dominant for all four steels; however, in the 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr samples, which contain long-range ordered nanodomains smaller than the critical size, σ_order also contributes. The 2Ni steel exhibits the largest precipitate sizes (approximately 2.0–4.8 nm) and the highest σ_coh+σ_mod values approaching 39 MPa, whereas 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr show smaller average precipitate radii and lower peak hardening. These results illustrate that Ni and Cr synergistically control precipitate size distributions and strengthening mechanisms, with Cr promoting fine LRO domains that contribute to ordering strengthening and Ni favoring larger κ-carbides that predominantly contribute coherency and modulus mismatch hardening.

Figure 11 is a stacked bar chart that compares the experimentally measured yield strengths with the calculated values derived from four additive strengthening mechanisms for Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steels alloyed with Ni and Cr. The x-axis lists four alloy compositions—3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr—while the y-axis reports yield strength in MPa. Each bar is divided into four color-coded segments: σ₀ (friction stress, orange), σgb (grain boundary strengthening, green), σss (solid solution strengthening, purple), and σprep (precipitation strengthening, yellow). Brown circular markers indicate the experimental yield strengths determined from tensile tests. Visually, σss provides the largest contribution (approximately 500–560 MPa) across all samples, whereas σprep varies markedly, being highest for 2Ni and lower for 3Cr, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr. The sum of the calculated contributions closely matches the experimental data points, validating the analytical superposition model. The figure supports the core conclusion that solid solution strengthening is the dominant mechanism due to the high 1.5% C content, while Ni and Cr synergistically control precipitation strengthening by altering precipitate type and size (κ-carbides versus LRO domains), thereby enabling targeted strength optimization in these lightweight steels.

The figure displays strain hardening rate versus true strain curves for Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steels, with a dark blue curve for 3Cr steel and a light blue curve for 2Ni steel. The 3Cr steel sustains a higher strain hardening rate near 2000 MPa up to failure at true strain ~0.4, while the 2Ni steel peaks near 1400 MPa and fails earlier near true strain ~0.25. Six circular schematic insets illustrate grain-level deformation microstructure evolution. For 3Cr steel, 3% strain (a) shows planar slip bands; 10% strain (b) shows high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA), cross-slip, and refined slip bands; large deformation (c, labeled LD) shows very fine slip bands, HDDA, deformation twins, and stacking faults. For 2Ni steel, 5% strain (d) and 10% strain (e) depict uniform slip band multiplication; large deformation (f, labeled LD) shows dense planar slip bands and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR), with caption reporting stacking faults near final failure but no deformation twins. The figure supports that Cr alloying promotes HDDA and DSBR, prolonging work hardening and enhancing ductility compared with the Ni-alloyed steel.
